exemplar.title: How could shoe sanitation products be improved in order to reduce risks of bacteria induced illnesses through the use of the design cycle and four pleasure framework?

detailedExemplarData.title: How could shoe sanitation products be improved in order to reduce risks of bacteria induced illnesses through the use of the design cycle and four pleasure framework?

strengths: Topic and purpose are communicated accurately and consistently throughout.
Research question is clearly stated, focused and effectively connected to every section.
Methodology draws on multiple informed choices (surveys, UV-C trials, prototyping) and is integrated into the discussion.
weaknesses: Some key methodological details are missing (e.g., control of UV-C intensity, survey sampling breakdown).
Occasional long or complex sentences reduce clarity in the introduction and background.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (topic communication and research question) fully meet topâband descriptors and the methodology, though very strong, had only minor omissions. Overall, the work demonstrates a coherent, focused design approach with complete and informed methods, warranting the maximum 6 out of 6.

strengths: Demonstrates excellent understanding of UV-C germicidal principles, E. coli biology and four-pleasure design theory.
Terminology (CFU, ÂµL, design cycle, etc.) is used accurately and consistently in context.
Sources are generally well integrated into tables, graphs and narrative to support key points.
weaknesses: Several citations are incomplete or missing, particularly around price assumptions and the four-pleasure framework.
Unit notation is occasionally inconsistent (e.g., âmicro litresâ vs âÂµLâ), and CAD drawings lack dimensional annotations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge is applied at a high level and terminology is largely precise. Minor citation gaps and notational inconsistencies prevent a perfect 6, but the overall depth and relevance of content justify a strong 5 out of 6.

strengths: Research data (market survey, lab trials, user feedback) is consistently and effectively applied to the design question.
Analysis compares exposure times and iterates on prototype placement, showing clear linkage to objectives.
Discussion critically acknowledges trial failures, trade-offs and safety issues, demonstrating evaluative depth.
weaknesses: Quantitative rigor is limited (no irradiance measurements or statistical analysis).
Modifications (e.g., bar placement) are not followed by re-testing data to confirm improvements.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs work shows strong critical thinking: research is highly relevant, analysis is well supported by multiple data sources, and evaluation is reflective and iterative. While more quantitative evidence would elevate it further, the quality of synthesis and critique places it firmly in the 7â9 band at 9 out of 12.

strengths: Report structure includes all required elements (title page, contents, sections, bibliography).
Layout is consistent, with properly numbered pages, word count and captioned figures.
weaknesses: A few figures are low-resolution and some captions overlap text.
Minor ordering issues (e.g., background before methodology) and typographical errors (e.g., âAccordion toâ¦â).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall the format and layout meet professional conventions, with only minor inconsistencies. The structure supports readability and presentation requirements at the top band, meriting 4 out of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Fonts, headings and tables are generally consistent; images are captioned and referenced. Word count, title and page numbering are provided. Nonetheless, several figures are low-resolution, some captions overlap text, and there are spacing/capitalisation inconsistencies. Overall presentation is competent but falls short of âprofessional,â justifying a 3 within the 3â4 band.

The student integrates market data, survey findings, lab tests and user feedback directly into design decisions, evidencing clear relevance to the research question. While some secondary sources are superficial marketing sites, the majority relate logically to germicidal efficacy or user needs. The breadth and repeated application justify a score toward the upper end of the 4â6 band.

The essay follows standard EE structure with title page, abstract substitutes, table of contents, sequential sections and bibliography. All required elements are present, yet minor mis-ordering (background before methodology), some duplicated headings and abrupt image placements disrupt flow. These shortcomings keep the work at the lower half of the top band.

The discussion recognises safety, cost and real-world contamination limitations, and evaluates design modifications against the four-pleasure goals. Still, evaluation remains partial; trade-offs (e.g., 30-minute cycle vs user convenience) are acknowledged but not fully explored. The quality corresponds to a competent but not highly critical discussion, positioning the work in the middle of the 4â6 range.

The research question â âHow could shoe sanitation products be improved â¦ through the use of the design cycle and four pleasure framework?â â is stated verbatim on the title page. It is sufficiently narrow (single product category, two specific design lenses) and consistently referenced in the market research, prototype iterations and conclusion. Although wording is a bit long, the focus remains tight enough to guide discussion, justifying placement in the top band.

The essay demonstrates sound knowledge of UV-C germicidal action, E. coli characteristics, design-for-pleasure theory and market positioning. Academic and commercial sources are cited and integrated into background, graphs and comparison tables. Nevertheless, citations are occasionally incomplete and some statements (e.g., pricing assumptions, environmental claims) lack corroboration. Depth is good but not consistently âexcellent,â meriting the middle band.

Key DT terminologyâCFU, UV-C wavelength, design cycle, user-centred design, four pleasure dimensionsâis used accurately and in appropriate contexts. Occasional grammatical errors and inconsistent unit notation (microliters vs Âµl) prevent a flawless presentation, but overall terminology is appropriate and mostly precise, fitting the 3â4 descriptor.

Analysis moves beyond description by comparing different exposure times, relocating shoe bars after interpreting petri-dish results, and critiquing survey price sensitivity. However, quantitative treatment is limited (no statistical calculations; visual inspection of colonies only) and some conclusions (âproduct highly feasibleâ) are asserted without cost analysis. This reflects âgoodâ but not âwell-supportedâ depth, earning a mid-band mark.

The student states the design problem, health context and userâexperience aims in the very first sentence, then reiterates them through the introduction, background and research question. Purpose (reducing shoe-borne bacteria by integrating the design cycle and the four-pleasure framework) is explicit and revisited in later discussion/evaluation. Minor language slips and some overly long sentences occasionally blur details, but overall the topic, scope and intended product improvements are communicated clearly and remain visible throughout the essay.

The student describes primary surveys, user interviews, agar UV-C trials and iterative prototyping. Selection of UV-C exposure times, agar medium and user-centred feedback indicates informed choices. However, important methodological details are missing or weak: no control for light intensity, limited explanation of sampling for 303-response survey, and no quantified criteria for four-pleasure assessment. Consequently, the methodology is âmostly completeâ but not fully rigorous, fitting solidly in the 3â4 band.

annotations.comment: Selection of design 4 is asserted but lacks explicit mapping of user feedback to specific four-pleasure dimensions. Detail this alignment to strengthen your rationale.
The feedback diagram clearly maps pleasure dimensions. Consider adding quantitative preference scores to illustrate the strength of each dimension.
CAD drawing is detailed but lacks dimensional annotations. Include measurements to demonstrate feasibility and manufacturing considerations.
Figure numbering is inconsistentâFig. 18 in caption then referenced as Fig. 19 elsewhere. Standardise numbering to avoid confusion.
The biochemical mechanism description is accurate, but integrating a mathematical expression (e.g., $$DNA\,damage \propto \text{dose}\times\text{time}$$) would enhance technical depth.
Use correct notation for bacterial volume. The expression $$E_{c^{O}}$$ appears incorrect; clarify and correct the subscripting of $$E. coli$$.
The title effectively communicates the design problem and research focusââHow could shoe sanitation products be improvedâ¦ââclearly establishing purpose and guiding the study.
The provided word count (3728) confirms adherence to the 4000-word limit requirement.
Table of contents is thorough but background precedes methodology, creating a slight structural jump. Reordering sections would improve flow.
The introductory paragraph uses overly long, complex sentences that hinder clarity. Consider dividing into shorter sentences for improved readability and focus.
The four-pleasure framework is introduced without citation. Provide a source for this model to strengthen the theoretical foundation.
Unit notation alternates between âmicro litresâ and âÂµlâ. Standardise to SI notation (e.g., 100 ÂµL) for precision and consistency.
The methodology lacks control of UV-C light intensity. Include irradiance measurements or a radiometer reading to validate consistent exposure.
The clinical lab results table is presented without contextual explanation or linkage to your design. Add discussion to highlight its relevance to your prototype objectives.
Price points for competitors are stated (e.g., 1,267 USD) without citation. Reference the source or clarify estimation method.
Typographical error in âAccordion to Grand View Researchâ. Correct to âAccording toâ to maintain professionalism.
The survey methodology is described only as âa survey was conductedâ. Include sample size, demographic breakdown and distribution method for completeness.
Charts are clearly presented but analysis is missing. Interpret key trends rather than solely displaying figures to deepen critical engagement.
The discussion uses percentages without reference to raw counts or statistical significance. Consider including confidence intervals or margins of error.
The transition to the second design caption is abrupt. Add a clear heading or paragraph break to distinguish between concepts.
Interview table is dense and hard to read. Increase cell spacing or reduce text per cell to enhance readability.
Table 1 appears empty, suggesting an oversight. Remove unused tables or populate with relevant data to avoid confusion.
The student acknowledges the first trialâs failure and identifies exposure time and inoculum density as factors. This demonstrates strong evaluative reflection.
Parameter adjustments are logical but lack quantitative justification. Include expected UV dose calculations for 15 min and 30 min exposures.
Adjustment to bar placement is evidence-based. However, no re-testing data is presented to confirm improved efficacy after modification.
User feedback table is informative but lacks thematic analysis. Summarise key themes or satisfaction scores to support evaluation.
The redesign allowing seated use shows strong user-centred focus and addresses key ergonomic concerns.
The identification of UV-C light leakage is insightful. Include quantitative light intensity readings around openings to validate and refine safety measures.
Claiming high market feasibility is premature without cost and production analysis. Consider integrating a basic cost breakdown.
Reference list exhibits inconsistent formatting and missing elements (e.g., author names, journal titles). Standardise to a single citation style.
